content.problemStatement:
Andrew likes meatballs very much as you know.

He has **N** plates of meatballs, here the **i**th plate contains exactly **i** meatballs. Andrew wants to take exactly **K** plates to his trip to Las Vegas.

On this occasion, he wants to choose the **K** plates by a strange way: if both **i**th and **j**th plates are chosen, then **i** and **j** must not be relative prime, for all **1 ≤ i < j ≤ N**.

Please help him to choose **K** plates. Output one of the possible choices.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two integers N and K separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output K distinct integers (1 ≤ value ≤ N) such that every pair of chosen numbers has gcd > 1. Numbers may be printed in any order and separated by spaces. If no such set exists, output a single integer -1. Each test case's answer is printed on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first case K=3 ≤ floor(100/2)=50, so we output the first three even numbers 2,4,6. All pairs share divisor 2. For the second case K=100 > 50, impossible, thus -1.
K=1, any single plate works; we output 1.
floor(7/2)=3, enough even numbers exist. The set {2,4,6} satisfies the condition.

solution.approach:
Observation: any two even numbers share the divisor 2, therefore a set consisting only of even numbers always satisfies the condition. The count of even numbers in [1, N] is floor(N/2). Hence:
- If K == 1 we can output any single plate (choose 1).
- Else if K ≤ floor(N/2) we output the first K even numbers: 2, 4, 6, …, 2*K.
- Otherwise it is impossible, because we would need more than floor(N/2) numbers, but at least one of them would be odd and would be coprime with some other odd number, breaking the rule. Thus we output -1.
The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T + total output size) – constant work per test case besides printing the answer.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
